description: The image displays a publisher interface icon labeled 'Check for updates' containing a circular emblem with a red bookmark, rather than a scientific materials characterization figure. No micrographs, diffraction peaks, mechanical curves, or quantitative data are visually discernible. Based on the supplied text extraction summary, the associated work studies Fe-20Mn-9Al-1.5C-2Ni-3Cr low-density steel subjected to single-stage heat treatments at 1000–1100 °C (S1000, S1050, S1100) and a multi-stage heat treatment (MHT: 1000 °C/3 min + 750 °C/15 min + 1050 °C/3 min). The text reports effective grain sizes of 3.17 μm (S1000), 3.84 μm (S1050), and 7.31 μm (MHT), alongside yield strengths of 955 MPa, 945 MPa, and 910 MPa respectively. Impact energies increase from 29.2 J (S1000) to 55.1 J (MHT), attributed to dissolution of intergranular Cr7C3 and a high proportion (~71.2%) of special grain boundaries promoting crack deflection. All materials-specific details are derived from the provided textual context and are not directly observable in the image.

This figure presents a schematic process flow diagram illustrating the complete thermomechanical processing route of Fe–20Mn–9Al–1.5C–2Ni–3Cr low-density steel, encompassing homogenization, hot rolling, and subsequent heat treatments. The primary flow shows that the cast ingot was first homogenized at 1200°C for 2 h, then hot-rolled at 1050–950°C to a 6 mm sheet, followed by air cooling to room temperature. After hot rolling, the route bifurcates into two parallel heat treatment strategies. The upper branch depicts single-stage heat treatments, where specimens were reheated to 1000°C (S1000), 1050°C (S1050), or 1100°C (S1100), held for 3 min, and quenched to room temperature. The lower branch details the multi-stage heat treatment (MHT): Stage I at 1000°C for 3 min with quenching, Stage II at 750°C for 15 min with quenching, and Stage III at 1050°C for 3 min with quenching. Each stage is visually distinguished by furnace heating and water-quenching icons. The diagram establishes the experimental design framework for comparing grain boundary engineering strategies—specifically, how an intermediate aging step prior to final austenitization is intended to dissolve detrimental intergranular precipitates and promote special boundary formation to enhance impact toughness without compromising high strength and ductility.

Figure 2 presents cross-sectional SEM micrographs (a–c) and EBSD grain-boundary maps with misorientation-angle distributions (d–f) for Fe–20Mn–9Al–1.5C–2Ni–3Cr low-density steel under three heat-treatment conditions: single-stage 1000 °C (S1000), single-stage 1050 °C (S1050), and multi-stage heat treatment (MHT). Panels (a–c) show equiaxed austenite grains containing annealing twins; S1000 (a) exhibits elongated Cr7C3 precipitates along grain boundaries, whereas S1050 (b) and MHT (c) display dissolved intergranular precipitates with MHT possessing a higher density of finer twins. Panels (d–f) map random high-angle boundaries and Σ ≤ 29 special boundaries (red lines), while insets reveal dominant ~60° misorientation peaks characteristic of coherent twin boundaries. Citation sentences report special-boundary proportions of 71.7 %, 68.3 %, and 71.2 % for S1000, S1050, and MHT, respectively, and note that MHT promotes increased twin nucleation during intermediate 750 °C aging, fragmenting the random boundary network to improve impact toughness without compromising strength.

This figure presents TEM and SAED micrographs characterizing precipitates in Fe–20Mn–9Al–1.5C–2Ni–3Cr low-density steel under three heat treatment conditions: single-stage 1000 °C (S1000, a–d), single-stage 1050 °C (S1050, e–h), and multi-stage heat treatment (MHT, i–l). Dark-field TEM images (a, e, i) reveal coherent nanoscale κ-carbide particles (3–10 nm) within the austenite matrix, indicated by white arrows. Corresponding SAED patterns (b, f, j) display faint superlattice diffraction spots confirming κ-carbide with an orientation relationship [011]γ // [011]κ. In S1000, bright-field TEM (c) shows elongated stripe-like Cr7C3 precipitates along austenite grain boundaries, indexed by SAED along [1-12]Cr7C3 (d). In S1050, grain boundary Cr7C3 dissolves, and bright-field TEM (g) captures B2 phase particles (20–160 nm), with SAED along [1-11]B2 (h). MHT samples similarly contain B2 (k), slightly smaller than in S1050, verified by SAED along [113]B2 (l). The results demonstrate that κ-carbide persists across all conditions, while intergranular Cr7C3 is eliminated at higher temperatures, with B2 emerging in S1050 and MHT.

Figure 4 presents the tensile mechanical response of Fe–20Mn–9Al–1.5C–2Ni–3Cr low-density steel under different heat-treatment conditions. Panel (a) shows engineering stress–strain curves for four specimens: multi-stage heat treatment (MHT), single-stage austenitization at 1000 °C (S1000), 1050 °C (S1050), and 1100 °C (S1100). S1000 and S1050 exhibit the highest yield and ultimate tensile strengths, both exceeding 1100 MPa with total elongations above 55 %. MHT displays marginally lower strength yet retains high ductility near 50 %, while S1100 shows the lowest strength but the largest elongation. Panel (b) plots true stress (solid lines) and strain-hardening rate (dashed lines) against true strain. The work-hardening rate evolves through three marked stages: an initial sharp decrease (Stage I), a rise to a peak of approximately 2000 MPa near true strain 0.20–0.22 (Stage II), and a final decline (Stage III). MHT, S1000, and S1050 exhibit similar hardening capacities, whereas S1100 shows a distinctly lower rate. These curves demonstrate that the optimized multi-stage heat treatment preserves a strength–ductility profile comparable to the best single-stage conditions, supporting grain-boundary engineering that improves toughness without sacrificing tensile performance.

Figure 5 presents instrumented Charpy impact test results for Fe–20Mn–9Al–1.5C–2Ni–3Cr low-density steel under three heat treatment conditions. Subfigure (a) shows load–displacement curves for S1000 (single-stage 1000°C), S1050 (single-stage 1050°C), and MHT (multi-stage heat treatment: 1000°C/3 min → 750°C/15 min → 1050°C/3 min, all quenched). MHT exhibits the largest displacement of about 8.04 mm and sustained load-bearing capacity, whereas S1000 fails earliest at about 4.55 mm with an abrupt load drop. Subfigure (b) decomposes total absorbed energy into crack initiation energy (red hatched) and crack propagation energy (blue hatched). The caption and text report total impact energies of 29.2 J, 42.8 J, and 55.1 J for S1000, S1050, and MHT, respectively. Crack initiation energies are comparable at approximately 16–18 J, while crack propagation energy increases markedly from about 9.9 J for S1000 to about 26.6 J for S1050 and about 36.8 J for MHT. This demonstrates that MHT dramatically improves toughness primarily by enhancing crack propagation resistance through grain boundary optimization and increased grain size, without compromising strength and ductility.

Figure 6 presents scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images of tensile fracture surfaces for Fe–20Mn–9Al–1.5C–2Ni–3Cr low-density steel under three heat treatment conditions. Each subpanel combines high-magnification local views (scale bar 10 μm) with low-magnification reduction maps (yellow-bordered insets, scale bar 0.5 mm) showing overall fracture morphology. Subpanel (a) shows sample S1000 (single-stage austenitization at 1000 °C for 3 min) exhibiting ductile dimple fracture with non-uniform dimple size. Subpanel (b) displays sample S1050 (single-stage austenitization at 1050 °C for 3 min) with similar dimpled morphology. Subpanel (c) reveals the multi-stage heat treated sample MHT (1000 °C/3 min + 750 °C/15 min + 1050 °C/3 min), where dimple features appear underdeveloped compared to S1000 and S1050, suggesting slightly reduced plasticity. The figure supports that while all samples fail via ductile mechanisms, the multi-stage treatment modifies fracture surface characteristics, correlating with retained strength and ductility while significantly improving impact toughness through grain boundary engineering.

Figure 7 presents scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images of Charpy V-notch impact fracture surfaces for Fe–20Mn–9Al–1.5C–2Ni–3Cr low-density steel under three heat-treatment conditions, with low-magnification overview insets (yellow borders). Subfigure (a) for sample S1000 (single-stage 1000 °C, 3 min) shows quasi-cleavage facets with slip bands, microcracks, and microvoids, indicating brittle intergranular fracture characteristics. Subfigure (b) for sample S1050 (single-stage 1050 °C, 3 min) exhibits similar quasi-cleavage features with microcracks and microvoids, representing a mixed intergranular and transgranular fracture mode. In contrast, subfigure (c) for sample MHT (multi-stage: 1000 °C/3 min + 750 °C/15 min + 1050 °C/3 min) displays a fully ductile dimpled rupture morphology with no quasi-cleavage planes and disappearance of microcracks. The transition from quasi-cleavage to dimpled fracture correlates with reported impact energies rising from 29.2 ± 2.0 J for S1000 to 55.1 ± 2.2 J for MHT, an 88.6 % improvement, demonstrating that the multi-stage heat treatment enhances toughness by promoting crack deflection through grain-boundary optimization without severely compromising strength.

This figure presents cross-sectional SEM micrographs of crack propagation regions in Fe–20Mn–9Al–1.5C–2Ni–3Cr low-density steel after three heat treatments: (a, b) single-stage 1000 °C (S1000), (c, d) single-stage 1050 °C (S1050), and (e, f) multi-stage heat treatment (MHT). Subfigures (a) and (b) show that S1000 contains intergranular Cr7C3 precipitates (circled in b) and aggregated intergranular micropores (yellow outlines in a), with cracks propagating primarily along grain boundaries. In S1050, (c) and (d) reveal cracks propagating across twins (yellow dashed lines) and undergoing deflection (white arrows in c), with a relatively straight crack path indicated by red dashed lines in (c). For MHT, (e) and (f) display pronounced zigzag cracking (red dashed lines in f) accompanied by repeated crack deflection and propagation across twins and special boundaries (yellow dashed lines). The comparative progression from intergranular cracking in S1000, to mixed-mode fracture with partial deflection in S1050, to tortuous zigzag paths in MHT illustrates that eliminating intergranular Cr7C3 precipitates while increasing special boundary density and grain size redirects crack propagation, increases effective crack length, and dissipates more energy, thereby raising impact toughness by 29%.

Figure 9 presents scanning electron microscopy (SEM) micrographs of the cross-section of Fe–20Mn–9Al–1.5C–2Ni–3Cr low-density steel after multi-stage heat treatment (MHT) incorporating an intermediate heat treatment at 750 °C. Panel (a), at lower magnification (scale bar 10 μm), shows equiaxed grains with a network of grain boundaries decorated by dark-contrast phases; a dashed white rectangle indicates the region enlarged in panel (b). Panel (b) offers a higher-magnification view (scale bar 2 μm) of the localized area, revealing closely spaced, discontinuous precipitates situated along the grain boundaries while grain interiors appear relatively clean. Note that the caption refers to (b) as low magnification, yet the scale bar confirms it is a magnified view of the boxed region in (a). These micrographs directly evidence extensive intergranular precipitation resulting from the 750 °C intermediate step. As the text reports, these grain-boundary precipitates elevate stress in non-twinned regions, generate more twin nucleation sites, and increase the frequency of special boundaries that disrupt random high-angle grain boundaries, thereby playing a key role in improving impact toughness while preserving strength and ductility.

The figure presents schematic illustrations comparing crack propagation mechanisms in Fe–20Mn–9Al–1.5C–2Ni–3Cr low-density steel under two heat treatment conditions. Panel (a) depicts the single-stage heat treated sample S1000 (1000°C for 3 min), where intergranular Cr7C3 precipitates are present along austenite (γ) grain boundaries. The crack propagates rapidly along random high-angle grain boundaries (marked R) with minimal deflection, facilitated by the embrittling effect of boundary precipitates. Panel (b) illustrates the multi-stage heat treated sample MHT (1000°C/3 min + 750°C/15 min + 1050°C/3 min), where the disappearance of intergranular precipitates and an increased frequency of special boundaries (marked Σ, coherent twin boundaries) force the crack to undergo repeated deflection and zigzag propagation across austenite grains. This increased crack path length enhances energy dissipation during crack propagation. The visual contrast supports the conclusion that optimizing grain boundaries via multi-stage heat treatment—specifically eliminating Cr7C3 and raising special boundary proportion to ~71.2%—transforms the fracture mode from rapid intergranular cracking to tortuous transgranular propagation, thereby improving impact toughness by 88.6% compared with S1000.
